perturbation.original_text: 10.1 NCC shall not be liable for any loss caused to the Owner or the Licensee either jointly or severally except for loss of or damage to the Material to the extent that such loss or damage is caused by the negligent acts or omissions of NCC, its employees, agents or sub-contractors and in such event NCC's total liability in respect of all claims arising under or by virtue of this Agreement shall not (except in the case of claims for personal injury or death) exceed the sum of (pounds)500,000.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.1 NCC shall not be liable for any loss caused to the Owner or the Licensee either jointly or severally, even for loss of or damage to the Material caused by potentially negligent acts or omissions of NCC, its employees, agents or sub-contractors, and in such event NCC's total liability in respect of all claims arising under or by virtue of this Agreement shall, at its sole discretion, not exceed a nominal sum.
perturbation.explanation: The original text limits NCC's liability to £500,000 for losses caused by negligence. The modified text attempts to exclude liability even for negligence ('potentially negligent acts or omissions') and introduces ambiguity by stating that liability will 'at its sole discretion, not exceed a nominal sum.' This makes the potential compensation unclear and heavily favors NCC.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 (UCTA), Sections 2 and 3
perturbation.law_explanation: Sections 2 and 3 of the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 (UCTA) prevent businesses from excluding or limiting liability for negligence that causes death or personal injury (Section 2) and from excluding or restricting liability for breach of contract unless the term is reasonable (Section 3). The modified clause's attempt to exclude liability for negligence and its vague description of 'nominal sum' would likely be considered unreasonable and unenforceable under UCTA, because it severely limits the licensee's ability to pursue damages for a potential breach and harm caused by negligence, contradicting UK law. A 'nominal sum' is not a defined amount which introduces additional unclarity to the deal.

Please see ‘Frequently Asked Questions’ for details regarding the timescales for which new effects are identified and recorded on this site. 2 Negligence liability. E+W+N.I. (1) A person cannot by reference to any contract term or to a notice given to persons generally or to particular persons exclude or restrict his liability for death or personal injury resulting from negligence. (2) In the case of other loss or damage, a person cannot so exclude or restrict his liability for negligence except in so far as the term or notice satisfies the requirement of reasonableness. (3) Where a contract term or notice purports to exclude or restrict liability for negligence a person’s agreement to or awareness of it is not of itself to be taken as indicating his voluntary acceptance of any risk. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a legal contradiction by attempting to exclude or severely limit liability for negligence, which is restricted by the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977. The original text limited liability to £500,000, while the changed text refers to a 'nominal sum' at NCC's 'sole discretion,' effectively negating potential compensation for negligence. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Section 2(2), states that 'a person cannot so exclude or restrict his liability for negligence except in so far as the term or notice satisfies the requirement of reasonableness.' The 'nominal sum' provision likely fails the 'reasonableness' test, creating a direct conflict with the UCTA.
